The five-level ladder from vague prompts to parallel agents — and why most users quit at level zero.
Every transformative tool in computing history has produced the same two crowds: the people who think it’s a miracle, and the people who think it’s a scam. Claude Code has both, in record numbers, right now. One crowd points at Anthropic’s own report that Claude now writes over 80% of the code merged into the company’s codebase. The other points at their own terminal, full of confidently broken output, and asks what everyone is smoking.
Here’s the resolution to that paradox, and it’s deflating for both camps: neither crowd is describing the tool. Both are describing their own skill level.
Magic and scam are two ways of saying the same thing — “I don’t understand the mechanism.” And when the mechanism is invisible, outcomes look random. They aren’t. Agentic coding has a learnable skill ladder, it has roughly five levels, and the difference between the miracle crowd and the scam crowd is usually nothing more than which rung they’re standing on.

Consider how every other serious developer tool introduces itself.
